free_text: The total neutron cross section of liquid and solid
ammonia
The Low Energy Neutron Source (LENS)
located within the Center for the Exploration of
Energy and Matter at Indiana University.
The detector was a 2.5 cm diameter tube
filled with 50 kPa 3He and 50 kPa N2, contained in a
3.81 cm square boron nitride shielding block into
which a 15.4 mm square aperture is cut as both
neutron beam inlet and beam outlet.
The ammonia was condensed into an aluminum cell with
a sapphire cover to allow visual inspection of the
sample morphology during condensation and cooldown.
The cell has a nominal volume of 0.716 ml, with a
thickness of 1.17+-0.01 mm, and is connected to a 980
ml staging tank and gas-handling systems. For the
experiment, authors introduced 812 mg of NH3 (6N
nominal purity from Airgas) into the system through
an Alicat MCS series mass flow controller at the
instrument floor's ambient temperature (roughly 290
K). The cell was then cooled to 218 K and the
pressure of the system dropped to 315 torr. At this
point the cell was 95% full (total charge roughly 480
mg), and the staging tank was isolated to avoid
overfilling the cell.
The transmission detector was positioned at
11.76+-0.02 m flight path length from the moderator
face. A cryostat positioned the sample in the beam at
a distance of 8.50+-0.01 m from the moderator face
and 3.26+-0.01 m from the 50 kPa 3He transmission
detector, which is mounted within a boron nitride
sleeve with 15 mm square aperture, and positioned
0.36 m in front of the SANS 2D detector. The incident
flux on the sample was monitored with a low-
efficiency 3He detector (4.6*10**-4 at 1A) located
5.25+-0.01 m from the moderator within a concrete
shielding bunker. The temperatures studied were 221 K
(for the saturated liquid), 180 K (solid, just
below the melting point) and 35 K (solid, far below
the melting point). After measurement at 221 K, the
sample was frozen and the temperature lowered to 180
K, substantially below the melting point of 195.4 K.
Following data collection at 180 K, the sample was
further cooled to 35 K, and the transmission measured
again.
The transmission cross sections were calculated using
ratios of counts from a full sample cell and an empty
sample cell, Cs and Ce, respectively, normalized by
similar counts from an upstream monitor counting (at
the same neutron energy) Ms and Me: sigma(E) =
(1/rhom)*ln((Ce(E)*Ms(E))/(Cs(E)*Me(E))), where rhom
is the areal density (in atoms or molecules/barn).
The resulting transmission must be scaled with an
areal number density to arrive at a microscopic cross
section. Should that areal density not be uniform
across the sample, corrections might need to be made,
especially when the transmission of the sample is low
or strongly varying in energy.
Statistical and systematic uncertainties.
Ammonia density variations across the sample are
significantly less than statistical uncertainty in
the measurement. The value of the areal number
density is not directly available for the solid
samples, as the in situ freezing process does not
ensure a full density solid that occupies the full
sample-cell thickness. Consequently, there is
considerable systematic uncertainty in the effective
density for our samples in the solid phase.
The magnitude of the energy correction is significant,
reaching as high as about 50% for energies greater
than 1 eV, but falling to only several percent for
energies less than 1 meV. The precise details of the
t correction are dependent on the details of the MCNP
calculation, including the fidelity of the moderator
model and the details of the scattering kernel(s)
used. Assuming transmission detector a
non-paralyzable dead time of 3.5 microsec required a
correction of less than 0.1% even in the time bins
with the greatest count rate, and authors therefore
ignored dead time correction in the analyses.
Approved by E.B.Iverson on March 16, 2026.
